Query: A 55-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-week history of tingling pain in the sole of his right foot when he raises it above chest level during exercises. He reports that he started exercising regularly 2 months ago and that his right calf cramps when he uses the incline feature on the treadmill, forcing him to take frequent breaks. The pain completely disappears after resting for a few minutes. He has an 8-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 34 years. His only medication is metformin. His pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 170/92 mm Hg. Straight leg raise test elicits pallor and tingling pain in the right foot. There is no pain in the back. His muscle strength is normal. Femoral pulses are palpable; right pedal pulses are absent. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

Options:
A: Femoropopliteal artery stenosis
B: Acute thrombosis of right popliteal vein
C: Lumbar spinal stenosis
D: Aortoiliac artery stenosis
"

Document [5] (Title: Peripheral Arterial Disease -- History and Physical) The most characteristic symptom of PAD is claudication which is a pain in the lower extremity muscles brought on by walking and relieved with rest. Although claudication has traditionally been described as cramping pain, some patients report leg fatigue, weakness, pressure, or aching. Symptoms during walking occur in the muscle group one level distal to the artery narrowed or blocked by PAD. Patients with aortoiliac artery occlusive disease, therefore, have symptoms in the thigh and buttock muscles while patients with femoropopliteal PAD have symptoms in their calf muscles. The walking distance at which symptoms occur depends on multiple factors including disease severity, walking pace, terrain, and incline.

Patients with aortoiliac disease frequently develop buttock pain.

Document [12] (Title: Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy(Archived) -- History and Physical) A thorough history and physical should be performed. All patients should be asked about a family history of HOCM and sudden cardiac death. A majority of patients are asymptomatic. Dyspnea is the most common complaint among symptomatic patients. Patients may also complain of presyncope, syncope, angina, palpitations (secondary to arrhythmia), or dizziness. Symptoms are frequently exacerbated by exertion. Severe cases will present, like congestive heart failure with paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, leg edema, and orthopnea. The most devastating presentation is sudden cardiac death. The physical exam does not provide a definitive diagnosis but should provide clues to increase clinical suspicion. Patients may present with a jugular venous pulse with prominent A wave, S4 heart sound, split-second heart sound (may paradoxically split in severe outflow tract obstruction), double carotid pulse, systolic ejection crescendo-decrescendo murmur, laterally displaced apical precordial impulse that may be abnormal forceful or enlarged, or a holosystolic murmur at the apex and axilla of mitral regurgitation with systolic anterior wall motion. The lung exam should be normal. The murmur and the gradient across the left ventricular outflow tract will decrease with an increase in preload (squatting) or afterload (handgrip). In addition, the gradient and the murmur will increase with a decrease in preload (Valsalva maneuver, diuretics, standing).
Document [13] (Title: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy -- History and Physical) Physical examination findings are characterized by a “jerky” pulse with rapid upstroke and downstroke, a normal S1 and split S2, an S3 due to decompensated heart failure, prominent “a wave” of jugular venous pressure, laterally displaced apical impulse, and double carotid pulse. An S4 is also common with LVH. A harsh mid-systolic ejection murmur at the left sternal border is affected as follows: Diminished intensity with increased preload (squatting) or afterload (handgrip) Increased intensity with a decrease in preload (Valsalva maneuver, standing) or with any reduction in afterload (vasodilator administration) Exercise will also increase the murmur. If mitral regurgitation is present, a holosystolic, blowing murmur may be loudest at the apex.

The reason for this absence of symptoms usually falls into 1 of 2 broad categories. Some patients with mild or moderate PAD rarely sustain a walking pace that increases the blood flow requirement of the lower extremity muscles. Other patients with PAD have muscle discomfort when they walk but fail to report these symptoms because they attribute them to the natural consequences of aging.

Document [25] (Title: Peripheral Vascular Disease -- History and Physical) Intermittent claudication is the most classic symptom of PAD, characterized by an exercise-induced cramping sensation with associated fatigue, weakness, and or pressure. It is not uncommon for patients to deny pain, and therefore asking about discomfort while ambulating is a more useful screening question. Symptoms are exacerbated by leg elevation and relieved by placing the limb in a dependent position. Paresthesias, lower extremity weakness, stiffness, and cool extremities may also be present. Anatomically the level of obstruction is usually seen one level above the area of discomfort; for example, patients with aortoiliac disease will have buttock and thigh symptoms. 70% to 80% of patients have stable intermittent claudication over 10 years; however, a portion of patients may progress through debilitating ischemic rest pain, critical limb ischemia, and eventual amputation. Document [31] (Title: Infrainguinal Occlusive Disease -- History and Physical) One of the most crucial parts of the examination and assessment is the ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI). The normal values are 1 to 1.3. A value of less than 0.9 suggests intermittent claudication, less than 0.5 is associated with rest pain, and less than 0.3 is critical limb ischemia. The ABPI is a highly sensitive test and is essential to form a diagnosis and assess disease severity. The ABPI may show a value of more than 1.3 in patients with diabetes due to calcification of the vessels. In that case, a segmental waveform analysis or toe-brachial index is an alternative.

These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2560) Elevated blood ketones in diabetic patients (ارتفاع الكيتونات في الدم عند مرضى السكري) are clinically associated with foot amputation (بتر القدم). Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb (Necrosis of amputation stump of right lower limb) is linked to amputation of the lower extremity and typically exhibits gangrene morphology (النَخَر). The broader category “amputation of lower extremity” encompasses specific procedures such as foot amputation (بتر القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة). Together, these connections illustrate how metabolic disturbances in diabetes can precipitate severe lower‑extremity outcomes, how necrotic changes at a stump often present as gangrene, and why early recognition and intervention are essential to prevent progression to major limb loss.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863) Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1742) Amputation of the lower extremity encompasses specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). Patients who undergo a lower‑extremity amputation may develop necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb, a complication that is morphologically linked to النَخَر and is directly associated with the amputation itself. Clinically, the occurrence of a lower‑extremity amputation is also associated with فشل قلبي احتقاني (congestive heart failure), highlighting the need for comprehensive cardiovascular assessment and monitoring in individuals requiring these surgical interventions.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926) Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1792) Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation), بتر القدم (foot amputation), and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). A notable complication of these lower‑extremity amputations is Necrosis of amputation stump of right lower limb, which is directly associated with the amputation of lower extremity and is morphologically described as النَخَر. This relationship underscores how the general procedure, its particular forms, and the potential necrotic outcome are interconnected in clinical practice.
